coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 co-modified with carbon dots and palladium nanocomposites for disinfection of bacterial and fungi
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jingtao
Liu, Shurui
Wang, Xueying
Yao, Jing
Zhai, Mengwan
Liu, Bingkun
Liang, Chengzhen
Shi, Hengzhen
coredata.dc:description: High efficiency photocatalysts capable of disinfecting a broad-spectrum microorganisms are needed for the practical application of photodisinfection technology. Herein, we synthesized a highly efficient photodisinfection catalyst composed of Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 decorated with carbon dots (CDs) and palladium nano-photocatalyst, designated as Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2, via a facile hydrothermal-calcination approach. XPS and ESR analyses were performed to verify that the composite contained Ti3+, while TEM imaging and FTIR confirmed that the samples contained CDs. The as synthesized photocatalysts, particularly the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 sample, exhibited superior photocatalyzed antibacterial activity to pure TiO2 against E. coli (～6.5 orders of magnitude increase at 30 min). The 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 photocatalyst also exhibited efficient photodisinfection of five pathogenic agricultural fungi. The dark cytotoxicity of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites was evaluated on HepG2 and Chinese hamster lung (V79) cells via Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and found to be minimal. Lastly, the recycling capacity for the photodisinfective activity of the nanocomposites was evaluated and found to be unchanged after five cycles. Four active species were identified as contributing to the photoinduced antimicrobial activity of the catalyst: h+, •O2 −, •OH, and e−. Together, our results indicate that Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites have great potential in agricultural plant pathogen disinfection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites and pure TiO2 nanoparticles after calcination at 200 ℃.
(a) High-resolution Ti 2p XPS spectrum. (b) High-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum. (c) High-resolution Pd 3d XPS spectrum. (d) ESR spectra of the pure TiO2 and Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites.
(a) High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum. (b) FTIR spectra of TiO2 and the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites.
(a) Optical absorbance and (b) PL spectra of Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites and TiO2 nanoparticles.

(a) Photocatalytic lethality on E. coli of Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites or pure TiO2 nanoparticles under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation. (b) Photocatalytic lethality on fungal spores: F. graminearum Schwabe, C. gloeosporioides Penz, B. dothidea, F. moniliforme Sheld and F. oxysporum f. sp. Vasinfectum of 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation.

(a) Recycling efficacy of photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites on E. coli under visible light irradiation. (b) XRD patterns of 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites before and after use in photodisinfection.
(a) ESR spectra of DMPO-O2•- and (b) DMPO-•OH adducts with 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite and visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation.

Photocatalytic lethality efficiency on E. coli by 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites in conjunction with various scavengers: 0.5 mM potassium dichromate, 0.5 mM sodium oxalate, 1 mM isopropyl alcohol, and 10 mM 4-hydroxy-TEMPO.

(a and c) Phase contrast microscope images of the corresponding fluorescent images (b and d) showing the cell viability of F. graminearum Schwabe macroconidia post 80 min illumination, (a and b) without photocatalyst or (c and d) with 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites. Cells were stained with CFDA, AM/Propidium Iodide: the live cells fluoresce green; the dead cells fluoresce.

Dark cytotoxicity of 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites evaluated by (a) V79 viability and (b) HepG2 viability via CCK-8 assay. Different letters above the curve indicate significant differences between treatments (p ＜ 0.05). Capital letters indicate significant differences between the differing concentrations of each group, while lowercase letters indicate significant differences between treatment durations.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 co-modified with carbon dots and palladium nanocomposites for disinfection of bacterial and fungi

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High efficiency photocatalysts capable of disinfecting a broad-spectrum microorganisms are needed for the practical application of photodisinfection technology. Herein, we synthesized a highly efficient photodisinfection catalyst composed of Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 decorated with carbon dots (CDs) and palladium nano-photocatalyst, designated as Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2, via a facile hydrothermal-calcination approach. XPS and ESR analyses were performed to verify that the composite contained Ti3+, while TEM imaging and FTIR confirmed that the samples contained CDs. The as synthesized photocatalysts, particularly the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 sample, exhibited superior photocatalyzed antibacterial activity to pure TiO2 against E. coli (～6.5 orders of magnitude increase at 30 min). The 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 photocatalyst also exhibited efficient photodisinfection of five pathogenic agricultural fungi. The dark cytotoxicity of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites was evaluated on HepG2 and Chinese hamster lung (V79) cells via Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and found to be minimal. Lastly, the recycling capacity for the photodisinfective activity of the nanocomposites was evaluated and found to be unchanged after five cycles. Four active species were identified as contributing to the photoinduced antimicrobial activity of the catalyst: h+, •O2 −, •OH, and e−. Together, our results indicate that Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites have great potential in agricultural plant pathogen disinfection.

ce:section.ce:para: Plant pathogens are a major cause of global agricultural crops yield loss, inducing not only a production decrease but also a reduction in nutritional quality (Beed, 2014; Savary et al., 2012). Moreover, fungal contamination of agricultural crops has direct health effects on humans and animals via mycotoxins (Tan et al., 2011). To combat these ill effects every year millions of tons of pesticide (Brauer et al., 2019) are applied the world over, about 26% of which are fungicides (Kah et al., 2019). However, due to the sustained use of these fungicides, many pathogenic agricultural fungi have acquired partial resistance to common chemical pesticides (Brauer et al., 2019; Chen, et al., 2016; Lucas et al., 2015; McGrath, 2001). As such, numerous alternate approaches—nanotechnology (Kah et al., 2019), genome editing (Zsögön et al., 2018) and photodisinfection (Foster et al., 2011; You et al., 2019) to name a few—have been attempted to combat agricultural fungi resistances. Among these, titanium dioxide (TiO2) based antifungal photocatalysts have attracted significant attention, due to their high physical and chemical stability, low cost, and superior photocatalytic efficiency (Naldoni et al., 2019; Rodríguez-González et al., 2019; Shayegan et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b).

The mechanism behind TiO2 photocatalysts’ efficacy is that the electron-hole pairs in TiO2 exhibit strong oxidative and reductive capacity when excited by a suitable light wavelength, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Wu et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). These highly unstable ROS then induce the mineralization/damage of cellular components, a breakdown of the cellular membrane, denaturation in cell-wall proteins, and direct DNA damage (Wu et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2013). However, TiO2 photocatalysts have two major drawbacks: poor visible-light utilization efficiency and low quantum yields. These limitations restrict the practical application of unmodified TiO2, and researchers have attempted numerous strategies—such as non-metal or metal doping, semiconductor coupling, or plasmonic metal incorporation—to overcome these drawbacks (Likodimos, 2018; Meng et al., 2019; Schneider et al., 2014).

Of the attempted strategies, self-doping is regarded as one of the most facile and efficient methods for enhancing TiO2 photocatalysts (Cao et al., 2019; Cho et al., 2019), since Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 has been shown to enhance visible-light utilization (Xiu et al., 2020). The introduction of Ti3+ or oxygen vacancies (Ov) into the TiO2 lattice narrows the band gap, improving visible-light absorbance and consequently photocatalytic performance (Chen et al., 2011; Zuo et al., 2010). While this approach tackles the TiO2 photocatalysts’ drawback of visible-light utilization efficiency, alternative approaches must be taken to address their low quantum yields.

One such approach to address the photocatalysts’ low quantum yields is to use TiO2 in a composite with another material, such as g-C3N4 (Pan et al., 2019), Fe2O3 (J. Zhang et al., 2018; P. Zhang et al., 2018), or carbon dots (CDs) (Shi et al., 2020). Among these, CDs have attracted the most interest due to their unique electronic properties, namely, efficient photoinduced electron transfer and a high electron reservoir capacity (Lim et al., 2015). CDs are a novel zero-dimensional carbon material with outstanding physical and chemical properties, such as high biocompatibility，low toxicity, good chemical stability, up-converting photoluminescence behavior, unique photoinduced electron transfer, and promising electron reservoir properties (Ding et al., 2020; Lim et al., 2015; Ye et al., 2020). The unique electronic properties of CDs enable them to act as electron acceptors or transporters, directing the flow of photogenerated charge carriers (Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b) and making them promising catalysis modifiers (Hu et al., 2019). Integration with CDs has been shown to enhance TiO2 photocatalysts’ performance via a reduction in photogenerated electron-hole recombination and an enlargement of the photo-absorptive region (Xu et al., 2019).

In our previous work we attempted to combine these two disparate approaches into one synergistic complex by preparing a Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 antifungal photocatalyst decorated with metallic copper (Cu/Ti3+-TiO2) (Zhang et al., 2020). While successful, we hypothesized that the released Cu ions that would accompany their wide spread adoption could be detrimental to the environment. Herein, we synthesized a nanocomposite photocatalyst of Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 via hydrothermal-calcination and utilized them for highly efficient disinfection of fungi and bacteria. The Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited superior visible-light absorption and excellent photodisinfective capability for E. coli and five pathogenic agricultural fungi, as compared with the pristine TiO2. In addition, we identified the dominant reactive photodisinfective species of the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst through ESR and ROS scavenging experiments, and its primary antifungal mechanism through fluorescence probing of intracellular esterase activity and the cell membrane/wall integrity. Lastly, our recycling photocatalytic disinfection experiments revealed that the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite is stable through multiple photodisinfections, validating its practical applicability.

Nanocomposites were synthesized via a solvothermal method followed by in air calcination following a previous methodology with slight modifications (J. Zhang et al., 2018; P. Zhang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). Briefly, 0.06 mmol palladium chloride was dissolved in 25 mL ethanol and underwent ultrasonication for 20 min. After sonication, 6 mmol titanium tetrachloride was added to the solution. Then, 30 mL NaOH ethanol solution was added at room temperature to obtain a fixed molar ratio of NaOH:TiCl4:PdCl2 at 4:1:0.01. The reaction proceeded for 20 min under vigorous agitation and a precipitate was observed. After which, 5 mL of n-propanol was added to the suspension before the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave flask and maintained at 180 ℃ for 4 h. Following the solvothermal reaction, the precipitate was washed three times (with distilled water and ethanol each) before it was dried at 60 ℃ overnight. Afterward, the precipitate was crushed into a fine powder and then calcined for 2 h in air at varying temperatures (200 °C, 300 °C, and 400 °C) to obtain the finished product (Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite). Differing atomic molar ratio of 0.3%, 0.5%, 2%, and 3% Pd were prepared according to above method by varying the starting Pd concentrations accordingly (all calcinated at 200 °C). Samples of TiO2 (no Pd) and Pd (no TiO2) as references were prepared in the same manner.

The nanocomposite’s crystal structure was obtained via powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation on a D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer (Bruker, Germany): scan range 2θ from 20° to 80° at a scan rate of 0.5°/min. Electron microscope images were acquired using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The ratios of Ti, C, Pd, and O in the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 photocatalyst was determined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., MA) with an Al K anode (1486.6 eV photon energy, 300 W). Ti3+ Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was measured at 110 K using a JES-FA200 electron spin resonance instrument (JEOL, Japan). The nanocomposite’s functional groups were analyzed via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Nicolet 6700 spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.), with a commercial TiO2 nanoparticle, Degussa P25, serving as a control. Diffuse reflectance measurements were used to determine the optical absorbance (U-3900 H UV–vis spectrophotometer, Hitachi Limited, Japan), with a BaSO4 reference. The steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalyst was determined at 385 nm excitation using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000, Japan), room temperature.

For the detailed protocols of E. coli and fungi culture see the included reference (J. Zhang et al., 2018; P. Zhang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). Briefly, Fusarium graminearum Schwabe, Colletotrichum gloeosporioides Penz, Botryosphaeria dothidea, Fusarium moniliforme Sheld, and Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. Vasinfectum were inoculated into solid potato dextrose agar (PDA) medium at 26 °C for 4 days. Fungi spores were then obtained using sporulation mung bean soup (MBS) medium at 26 °C for an additional 2–4 days on a rotary shaker at 120 rpm. Before use in photocatalytic inactivation experiments, the macroconidia suspension was diluted (final concentration ~2 × 106 CFU mL−1, distilled water). The E. coli cells were cultured in liquid Luria−Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C for 12 h. As above, before use the E. coli cell suspension was diluted (final concentration ~2 × 107 CFU mL−1, PBS). All materials were autoclaved at 121 °C for 30 min before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic inactivation of E. coli and fungal spores
ce:section.ce:para: For the following experiments a fixed concentration of 1 mg photocatalyst per 1 mL E. coli or spore suspension was used. 10 mg of the photocatalyst in 9.9 mL buffer solution was injected into a sterile 60 mm × 15 mm Petri dish and ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. After which, 1 mL of the fungal spore suspension (ca. 106 CFU/mL) was added to the Petri dish (starting concentration 105 CFU/mL). For E. coli cells, 0.1 mL of the cell suspension (ca. 109 CFU/mL) was added to the Petri dish (starting concentration 107 CFU/mL). A cooling water circulation device was used to maintain constant temperature during the photodisinfection progress.

The light source for the photocatalytic inactivation experiments was a 300 W xenon lamp with a UV (λ > 400 nm) cut-off filter (MicroSolar300, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co. Ltd., China), with the intensity of light upon cells measured to be ca. 5 mW/cm2 (FZ-A optical radiometer, Photoelectric Instrument Factory of Beijing Normal University, China). At periodic intervals a 0.1 mL aliquot of cell suspension was removed for evaluation. For the E. coli cells the 0.1 mL aliquots were diluted with PBS (pH 7) to the appropriate multiple, spread onto a LB agar plate, and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. For the fungal spores, the 0.1 mL aliquots were diluted as above and then spread onto a PDA agar plate and incubated at 28 °C for 48 h. After the corresponding incubation duration, viable cells (i.e colony-forming units) were counted. Dark control experiments were performed with no light sources but were otherwise under identical conditions.

For the radical scavenger studies, one of four radical scavengers—0.5 mM sodium oxalate, 0.5 mM potassium dichromate, 10 mM 4-hydroxy-TEMPO, and 1 mM isopropyl alcohol—were added into the above reaction system, and otherwise followed identical conditions. All studies were conducted in triplicate.

The formation of •OH and •O2 −, were characterized via electron spin resonance (ESR) at room temperature on a JES-FA200 (JEOL, Japan): working in X-band mode at 9055.244 MHz, center field 322.767mT, and 0 dB power. ESR signals are measurements of the radicals’ spin trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). The impact of the involvement of •OH was evaluated in ultrapure water, whereas, •O2 − was examined in methanol due to •O2 −’s instability in water. 50 mg of the photocatalyst was suspended in 50 mL DMPO solution (100 mM) for these reactions. Spectra were recorded at 0, 5, and 10 min during irradiation (300 W xenon lamp, λ > 400 nm UV cut-off filter).

To evaluate the vitality of the fungal spores, with or without photocatalytic treatment, a FungaLight CFDA, AM/Propidium Iodide Yeast Vitality Kit (Molecular Probes, USA) was used (Mukherjee et al., 2020). In this assay, the acetoxymethyl ester (AM) of the esterase substrate 5-caresterases diacetate (CFDA) enables the reagent to permeate cell membranes. Once inside, the diacetate groups are cleaved by esterase, resulting in a green fluorescence. The charged form of CFDA then leaks very slowly out of the cells, thereby, acting as a membrane integrity indicator. Concurrently, propidium iodide (PI) can only penetrate cells with damaged membranes. Utilizing a proper mixture of CFDA-AM and PI stains, esterase active cells with intact cell membranes will stain fluorescent green, whereas, cells with damaged membranes will stain fluorescent red.

In this experiment CFDA-AM (10 mg/mL) and PI (0.1 mg/mL) were respectively dissolved in DMSO, and kept at in the dark at −20 °C. 1 μL of each sample was then added into 1 mL of cell samples from the photocatalytic experiments and incubated in the dark for 30 min at 37 °C. After which, samples were washed with PBS and resuspended in 50 μL of PBS. A 20 μL suspension was dropped onto a glass slide and then covered with a clear glass coverslip for imaging (Nikon Ni-E upright microscope, DS-Ri2 camera, NIS-Elements AR Image software; Nikon, Japan).

To evaluate the dark toxicity of the nanocomposite, the viability of V79 and HepG2 cells were evaluated via CCK-8 assay at varying composite concentrations. Cells were seeded in a 96-well culture plate at the logarithmic phase (100 μL, 104 cells/mL) and cultured overnight at 37 °C, 5% CO2. Several concentrations of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.8 mg/mL in DMEM) were added to cells in the treatment group, with the control group receiving an equal quantity of PBS. The cell culture medium was replaced with fresh medium (without the nanocomposite) after 24 or 48 h. 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was then added to each well plate and the cells were further incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. After which, their absorbance at 450 nm was determined (iMark microplate reader, BIO-RAD, USA). All tests were performed under dark conditions and in triplicate. Results were evaluated using analysis of variance with statistical significance at p < 0.05.

Fig. S1a shows the XRD of samples containing 1% Pd and calcined at differing temperatures (200 °C, 300 °C and 400 °C) for 2 h in air. The anatase phase of TiO2 was observed in every sample, regardless of calcination temperature (Barba-Nieto et al., 2020). After calcination at 200 °C, only a Pd diffraction peak was clearly observed and no PdO diffraction peak could be observed (Gao et al., 2020). However, in the sample calcined at 300 °C both the Pd and PdO diffraction peaks could be seen. Moreover, the main diffraction peak of PdO was notably enhanced with calcination temperatures of 400 °C. As shown in Fig. S1b, the pilot photodisinfection study demonstrated that samples calcined at 200 °C had the best performance, with all E. coli cells inactivated after 40 min treatment. Based on these pilot study results, all future samples were calcined at 200 °C. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of pure TiO2 as well as nanocomposites with varying quantities of palladium. The anatase phase of TiO2 was observed in each sample, excepting those with brookite and rutile. The XRD peak of Pd is clearly delineated, with the signal strengthening accordingly with the atomic ratio of Pd/Ti. In a previous study, Kuvarega et al. reported that the diffraction peak of PdO was enhanced with increased of Pd content (Kuvarega et al., 2011). As in Fig. S1a, no diffraction peak of PdO was be observed in Fig. 1.

The Ti 2p XPS spectra for the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite ( Fig. 2a) (Guo et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020) was used to determine the binding energies at 464.7 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 463.9 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), 458.7 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2), and 458.1 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2). In Fig. 2b, the peak at 530 eV can be ascribed to the O lattice of TiO2, while the peak at 531.8 eV can be ascribed to oxygen vacancies (Qin et al., 2020; A. Wang et al., 2020; H. Wang et al., 2020; K. Wang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 2c, four peaks at binding energies of 342.2 eV, 340.5 eV, 336.9 eV and 335.2 eV were observed, demonstrating that palladium exists as Pd0 and PdO in the nanocomposite post calcination at 200 °C (Gao et al., 2020). This result was confirmed by comparison to a control sample of Pd; in Fig S2 the main diffraction peak of Pd can be clearly observed. In addition, the subordinate diffraction peak of PdO was enhanced post calcination. It is thusly implied that PdCl2 was reduced to Pd0 during the solvothermal reaction.

ESR is a technique particularly suited for detecting Ti3+ species, owing to its high sensitivity towards species with unpaired electrons (Rajaraman et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 2d, the sharp steep signal at g = 1.999 indicates the presence of Ti3+ in the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite (Kuvarega et al., 2011; F.L. Wang et al., 2019; Y. Wang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the ESR signal gradually enhances in proportion to increasing Pd quantity, suggesting Pd benefits for the formation of Ti3+. Although the identification parameters for Ti3+ species and oxygen vacancies overlap at times (Rajaraman et al., 2020), the work by Wang et al. shows that Ti3+ ESR signals present at g= 1.999 (F.L. Wang et al., 2019; Y. Wang et al., 2019). The C 1s spectrum ( Fig. 3a) of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite was divided into three peaks: 284.6 eV (C-C), 286.3 eV (C-O), and 288.8 eV (C˭O) (Li et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020). Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of pure TiO2 and the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite. Two characteristic peaks observed at 1240 and 1580 cm−1 in the nanocomposite spectra can be attributed to typical stretching vibrations for C-O and C˭O, respectively (Han et al., 2020). The 2845–2975 cm−1 peak is typical of C-H vibrations. No characteristic CD FTIR peaks were observed in the TiO2 or P25 controls. The band at 3480 cm−1 in the nanocomposite spectra is typical of stretching vibrations for adsorbed water (Han et al., 2020).

Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of the synthesized materials in air revealed wt% losses for the P25, 0.3%, 0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 3% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 of 0.91%, 2.53%, 2.99%, 3.39%, 3.11%, and 3.05%, respectively (Fig. S3). Weight-loss below 200 °C was attributed to the removal of adsorbed water, accounting for ~2.31 wt% in the case of 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 (Ramimoghadam et al., 2014). These TG results confirmed the presence of CDs on TiO2 NPs. In previous reports, CDs decoration was found to improve the charge separation efficiency of TiO2, which might be beneficial for catalysis. (Mahmood et al., 2021; F.L. Wang et al., 2019; Y. Wang et al., 2019).

The morphology and microstructure of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite was further characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S4, two sizes of nanoparticle, ~20 nm and ~5 nm, were observed. HRTEM images of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite illustrated lattice spacings of 0.35 nm and 0.22 nm, which were ascribed to the in-plane reflection of the (1 0 1) lattice plane of TiO2 (Barba-Nieto et al., 2020) and the (111) lattice plane of Pd (Gao et al., 2020), respectively. A set of lattice planes with a d-spacing of ~0.21 nm were also observed and ascribed to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon (Sun et al., 2020; A. Wang et al., 2020; H. Wang et al., 2020; K. Wang et al., 2020). These HRTEM images indicate that the nanocomposite indeed contained CDs. Although, the mechanism of CD formation in these conditions is unclear, based on the results of XPS, FTIR, and TEM, CDs were successfully synthesized. In order to further confirm formation of Pd0 and CDs, the XRD and TEM of the pure Pd calcined at 200 °C was evaluated (Fig. S2 and Fig. S5). In Fig. S2, Pd diffraction peaks can be clearly observed regardless of sample calcination. In Fig. S5, the bone like Pd as well as the CDs can be clearly observed, verifying that Pd0 and CDs were synthesized.

The photoactive characteristics of the nanocomposite were then investigated via UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Pure TiO2 exhibited characteristics indicative of a relatively high absorbance and a wide bandgap in the UV range ( Fig. 5a). Conversely, the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 composite absorbance shifted into the visible light range. Additionally, as Pd atomic ratio increased, the nanocomposite’s visible light absorbance increased as well, a result in agreement with the above ESR studies (Fig. 2d). The PL analysis evaluated the separation efficiency of the photoinduced electrons and holes in the photocatalysts. A reduced fluorescence emission intensity is indicative of a higher separation efficiency and accordingly enhanced photoactivity. Fig. 5b illustrates the PL spectra of the pure TiO2 and all the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites post 385 nm excitation. The addition of Pd first decreased the PL intensity, then increased the intensity when up to 1% Pd was used.

Previous reports have highlighted that transition metal ion additives play complex roles in TiO2 photocatalytic activity (Li et al., 2007; Choi et al., 1994; Subramanian et al., 2001). In addition to acting as a charge-trapping centers, they can also serve as charge recombination centers and affect charge release and migration. In the paper by Choi et al., the ability of a metal ion additive to function as an effective trap in TiO2 was dopant concentration dependent. Therefore, although stronger visible-light absorbance may be achieved by increasing the Pd additive concentration, the relationship between visible-light absorbance and photocatalytic efficiency is not a simple linear correlation. Herein, the higher Pd amounts (2% and 3%) may create new centers of electron hole recombination, enabling a higher PL intensity but reduced efficacy compared to the 1% sample.

The nanocomposite’s photocatalytic efficiency was defined as its visible light (ca. 5 mW/cm2) induced antimicrobial inactivation efficiency on a prokaryotic bacteria (E. coli) and five agricultural pest fungal spores. The E. coli survival ratio, with or without the nanocomposite, are shown in Fig. 6a. After 40 min of visible light irradiation alone, E. coli’s survival ratio was unchanged. After 40 min light irradiation with pure TiO2 the survival ratio was reduced to ~47%, indicating moderate cellular photodamage. In the dark control, after 40 min treatment with 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite the survival ratio of E. coli was ~6.3%. These cells deaths were likely triggered by Pd or CD nanoparticles accepting electron from cell membranes (Vijilvani et al., 2020; Xin et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019). The antibacterial ability of the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite was remarkably enhanced under visible light illumination. 20 min visible light irradiation with 0.3%, 0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 3% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites produced 1.3, 2.1, 5.5, 4.9, and 3 log10 CFU/mL viable E. coli cells, respectively (Fig. 6a). The survival ratio decreased to 1.0 × 10−7 log10 CFU/mL once the irradiation time was increased to 30 min, and no E. coli colony was detected once the duration was increased to 40 min on plants treated with 1%, 2%, or 3% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposites; 6.5 orders of magnitude lower than those treated with pure TiO2. Among the tested formulations, the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited the best disinfection efficiency, which is in agreement from the PL intensity examination. For context, in a previous work, Huang et al. found that all Klebsiella pneumoniae cells (107 CFU/mL) could be inactivated after 400 min irradiation of 0.1 mg/mL palladium-C-codoped TiO2 under 7.25 mW/cm2 visible light (Huang et al., 2020).

The photodisinfective capabilities of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite were further validated against pathogenic agricultural fungal spores. After 80 min visible light irradiation alone, minimal damage was observed in B. dothidea (survival ratio 79.6%). This minor damage is likely due to direct cellular photodamage from light illumination. In the dark controls, the following fungal spore survival ratios were observed (Fig. S6): F. graminearum Schwabe (69.2%), C. gloeosporioides Penz (74.8%), F. oxysporum f. sp. Vasinfectum (60.6%), F. moniliforme Sheld (65.2%), and B. dothidea (82%). No fungal spore surpassed a 1 log10 CFU/mL reduction in the dark controls. This result is agreement with our previous study (Zhang et al., 2020). The dark antifungal effect of our 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite is likely induced by the Pd or the CDs, as with the E. coli above. After 80 min visible light irradiation, the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite lead to the following surviving fraction of fungal spores (Fig. 6b): 3.7 log10 CFU/mL (F. graminearum Schwabe), 3.9 log10 CFU/mL (C. gloeosporioides Penz), 4.7 log10 CFU/mL (F. oxysporum f. sp. Vasinfectum), 4.8 log10 CFU/mL (F. moniliforme Sheld), and 5 log10 CFU/mL (B. dothidea). These rates were significantly faster than those exhibited in our previous report, even with the reduced light intensity (Zhang et al., 2013, 2020). In our previous report, TiON/PdO nanoparticles and a Cu/Ti3+-TiO2 composite reduced the surviving fraction of F. graminearum Schwabe spores to 3.5 and 2.7 log10 CFU/mL, after treatment for 5 h under 20 mW/cm−2 and 100 min under 80 mW/cm−2 light, respectively. Notably, all B. dothidea fungal spores were inactive post 60 min visible light irradiation (Fig. 6b). The differing antifungal performance of the photocatalyst depending on fungal spore varietal may be caused by differences in surface charge (Douglas and Collins, 1959; Zhang et al., 2013). Huang et al. found that all the Aspergillus niger spores (105 CFU/mL) could be inactivated within 132 h irradiation of 1 mg/mL palladium-C-codoped TiO2 under 7.25 mW/cm2 visible light (Huang et al., 2019). Photocatalytic performance against bacteria or fungi is affected by numerous parameters: UV or visible light intensity, material dosage, and microbial cell concentrations (He et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2020). From one experiment to another, a reduction in light intensity, material dosage, contact time, or a higher initial cell concentration are all indicators of an improvement in antimicrobial properties beyond a simple increase in total microorganism lethality. Compared with recent reports, our material exhibited superior anti-microorganism efficiency and in reduced time over comparable materials (Table S1).

To confirm the nanocomposites stability after repeated use, recycling photocatalytic disinfection experiments on E. coli were performed. As shown in Fig. 7a, the photodisinfective activity of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited no significant decrease after five cycle runs, each lasting 30 min. XRD of the composite after five cycles exhibited that its crystal structure remained unchanged as well (Fig. 7b). The composite is thus stable throughout the photocatalytic process and its reuse is viable.

Ti3+ has been shown to contribute to band gap narrowing (Lan et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020) and, as hypothesized in the introduction, Pd and CDs may function to trap photogenerated electrons and advance the photoinduced separation of holes and electrons (Hutton et al., 2017; A. Wang et al., 2020; H. Wang et al., 2020; K. Wang et al., 2020). Thereby, enabling the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite to have both oxidative and reductive capabilities. To examine this hypothesis, the photoreaction oxidation and reduction products—hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −)—in the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite were evaluated using DMPO spin-trapping ESR. No signals were detected for either ESR spectrum in under dark control ( Fig. 8). However, signals for both DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH were detected after 5 min visible light irradiation. Furthermore, these signals increased along with an increasing irradiation time (10 min). These results indicate that both •OH and •O2 − are produced in a light dependent manner from the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite, and moreover, that these species contribute to the photodisinfective activity of the nanocomposite.

In addition to •OH and •O2 −, the photogenerated holes (h+) and electrons (e−) could be have a beneficial impact on photocatalytic microbial inactivation. In order to elucidate which of these species plays the most impactful role in the nanocomposites’ disinfective capabilities, the effect of four radical scavengers: sodium oxalate (h+), potassium dichromate (e−), isopropyl alcohol (•OH), and 4-hydroxy-TEMPO (•O2 −), were evaluated (Zeng et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 9, the scavengers decreased the reaction rate in the following order: sodium oxalate > 4-hydroxy-TEMPO > isopropyl alcohol > potassium dichromate. These results indicate a critical role for h+ in the photo-antimicrobial efficacy of the nanocomposite, as well as a significant role for •O2 −. A possible mechanism for their relative impacts could be the high photogeneration efficiency for electrons trapped by the Pd/CDs, which consequently produce •O2 − species inducing disinfection unless inactivated directly via e-. Notably, microbial inactivation efficiency was also inhibited, albeit less significantly than by sodium oxalate or 4-hydroxy-TEMPO, with the addition of isopropyl alcohol and potassium dichromate. These results indicate that each h+, •O2 −, •OH, and e- contribute to the photocatalytic microbial inactivation of the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite. This four radical disinfective modality would be congruent with the highly efficient photocatalysis inactivation of bacteria and fungus observed previously (Fig. 6).

The cell well/membrane integrity, cell vitality, and intracellular protease activity were all evaluated through fluorescent staining via the FungaLight CFDA, AM/Propidium Iodide Yeast Vitality Kit. All F. graminearum Schwabe spores were found to be stained green after 80 min visible light irradiation with no photocatalyst, indicating visible light has minimal inherent toxicity to the spore ( Fig. 10b). Conversely, when treated with the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite and visible light irradiation, all spores were stained red, indicating cell membrane damage and spore inactivation (Fig. 10d). These staining results agree with the above plating results (Fig. 6b). Fig. 6a and c exhibit the corresponding phase contrast microscope images of Fig. 6b and d. As can be seen in Fig. 6c, the spores retained their structure after treatment although despite their loss in viability.

The survival rate of mammalian cells after co-culture with the nanocomposite was then evaluated as a safety indicator for the nanoparticles (Son et al., 2015). The cytotoxicity of the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite was evaluated via CCK-8 assay on V79 and HepG2 cell viability. CCK-8 examination revealed that the viability of HepG2 and V79 cells were only minimally reduced after 24 or 48 h of dark incubation with the nanocomposite (survival ratio 24/48 h: HepG2 86.4%/86.6%, V79 89.2%/84.5% at 0.8 mg/mL nanocomposite; Fig. 11). While these results indicate general safety to mammalian cells, it is worth noting that there were significant differences in viability depending on the nanocomposite dose and exposure duration, indicating further safety examinations are needed.

Herein we have synthesized a carbon dot and Pd co-modified Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst via hydrothermal-calcination. Characterization assays confirmed the presence of carbon dots, Pd, and Ti3+ ions in the nanocomposite. When compared to pure TiO2, the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite displayed superior visible light induced antimicrobial activity against E. coli. The 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite formulation, in particular, exhibited highly efficient photocatalyzed antimicrobial properties. Furthermore, the 1% Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite displayed excellent visible light induced antimicrobial activity on five pathogenic fungal spore varieties. To determine the mechanism behind this antifungal efficacy four likely radicals were examined (h+, •O2 −, •OH and e−) and each was found to contribute to the photocatalytic disinfection. Further mechanistic investigation by fluorescent staining via CFDA-AM/PI revealed that spore membranes/walls were damaged when incubated with the nanocomposite under visible light. A recycling test indicated the composite was stable during the photocatalytic process and can be reused for photodisinfection. Lastly, cytotoxicity evaluation in HepG2 and V79 cells indicated minimal toxicity from the composite. Our results suggest that the Pd/CDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite has exciting potential for plant pathogen disinfection in agricultural areas.
